qa.user_verbatim_complaint: Fix the LOCK_FILE EISDIR stderr leak in hooks/userprompt-tmpfs-pressure.sh at line 129. Change `} 9> "$LOCK_FILE" 2>/dev/null` to `} 2>/dev/null 9> "$LOCK_FILE"`.

spec_section_updates.section_4: hooks/userprompt-tmpfs-pressure.sh line 129 now contains `} 2>/dev/null 9> "$LOCK_FILE"` (redirect order: stderr-to-null first, fd-9 lock second). bash -n exits 0. All 4 pytest tests pass (AC_SRC, AC7c, AC7_regression, AC7b_regression).

qa.complaint_resolution_evidence: grep confirms `} 2>/dev/null 9> "$LOCK_FILE"` present at line 129. bash -n exits 0. AC7c runtime harness (LOCK_FILE pre-created as directory) verifies hook exits rc=0, stdout='', stderr='' — confirming the EISDIR stderr leak is silenced.
qa.overall_assessment: Single-line redirect reorder at hooks/userprompt-tmpfs-pressure.sh:129 is correctly applied. The pattern `} 2>/dev/null 9> "$LOCK_FILE"` is present at line 129. bash -n exits 0. All 4 pytest tests pass: AC_SRC (source check + syntax check), AC7c (LOCK_FILE-as-directory EISDIR harness), AC7_regression, and AC7b_regression. No band-aid patterns detected — the fix is the minimal correct reorder that matches Bash left-to-right redirect evaluation semantics. No regressions introduced.

success_criteria_results.verification_method: grep of literal pattern at line 129; bash -n syntax check
success_criteria_results.evidence: grep -n '} 2>/dev/null 9> "$LOCK_FILE"' hooks/userprompt-tmpfs-pressure.sh -> line 129 match. bash -n exit code 0.

success_criteria_results.source_phrase: Add AC7c to cover this path
success_criteria_results.criterion: AC7c: LOCK_FILE pre-created as directory; hook exits rc=0, stdout='', stderr=''
success_criteria_results.verification_method: pytest test_ac7c_lockfile_eisdir_stderr_silenced — sets up LOCK_FILE as directory, runs hook, asserts rc=0 stdout='' stderr=''
success_criteria_results.evidence: pytest output: 4 passed in 0.13s; AC7c PASSED

root_cause_verification.rationale: The root cause is Bash left-to-right redirect evaluation: `9> "$LOCK_FILE"` before `2>/dev/null` means fd-9 open failure (EISDIR) emits to real stderr before the null-redirect takes effect. The fix reverses the order so `2>/dev/null` silences stderr before `9>` is evaluated. AC7c runtime harness confirms rc=0, stdout='', stderr='' when LOCK_FILE is pre-created as a directory. Only line 129 was changed; all other lines are unmodified.
